questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Soft iron is utilized for making electromagnets since it possesses
options: low retentivity and high coercive force
low retentivity and low coercive force
high retentivity and high coercive force
high retentivity and low coercive force
questions.solution:
Electromagnets are temporary magnets that need to be strongly magnetized when current flows through the coil and completely demagnetized when the current is switched off.

To ensure that the magnetism effectively drops to zero when the current is removed, the core material must have low retentivity.

Additionally, the material should be easily magnetized and demagnetized with minimal energy loss (low hysteresis loss), which requires a low coercive force.

Soft iron is utilized for making electromagnets because it possesses high magnetic permeability, low retentivity, and low coercive force.

Answer: low retentivity and low coercive force

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a ferromagnetic material is subjected to an external magnetic field, its magnetic domains
options: may either expand or shrink in size
questions.solution:
When a ferromagnetic material is placed in an external magnetic field, the domains whose magnetic moments are aligned parallel or at a small angle to the direction of the external field expand in size.

Conversely, the domains whose magnetic moments are aligned opposite or at a large angle to the external field shrink in size.

Therefore, the magnetic domains may either expand or shrink in size depending on their orientation relative to the applied magnetic field.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A current $i$ is carried by a long, straight wire. The magnetizing field intensity $H$ is determined at a point $P$ close to the wire. A long, cylindrical iron rod is then brought near the wire such that point $P$ is located at the centre of the rod. The value of $H$ at point $P$ will
questions.solution:
The magnetizing field intensity $H$ at a distance $r$ from a long straight wire carrying current $i$ is given by Ampere's law:

$\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = i_{free}$

$H = \dfrac{i}{2\pi r}$

The value of the magnetizing field $H$ depends only on the free current $i$ and the distance $r$. It is independent of the magnetic medium present. When a cylindrical iron rod is placed such that point $P$ is at its centre, the magnetic induction $B$ at $P$ increases many times because $B = \mu_r \mu_0 H$ and iron has a high relative permeability ($\mu_r \gg 1$). However, since the free current $i$ and the distance $r$ remain unchanged, the magnetizing field intensity $H$ remains almost constant.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Negative magnetic susceptibility is exhibited by
questions.solution:
Magnetic susceptibility $\chi$ is defined as the ratio of the intensity of magnetization $M$ to the magnetic intensity $H$.

For diamagnetic materials, the induced magnetization is in the direction opposite to the applied magnetic field. Therefore, they have a small and negative magnetic susceptibility ($-1 \le \chi < 0$).

For paramagnetic materials, the susceptibility is small and positive ($\chi > 0$).

For ferromagnetic materials, the susceptibility is large and positive ($\chi \gg 1$).

Thus, negative magnetic susceptibility is exhibited by diamagnetic materials only.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A paramagnetic material is placed within a magnetic field. The field is elevated until the magnetization attains a constant value. If the temperature is subsequently lowered, the magnetization will
questions.solution:
When the magnetic field is elevated until the magnetization attains a constant value, the paramagnetic material achieves magnetic saturation.

At magnetic saturation, all the atomic magnetic dipoles are perfectly aligned in the direction of the external magnetic field, and the magnetization reaches its maximum possible value, $M = N \mu$, where $N$ is the number of atoms per unit volume and $\mu$ is the magnetic moment of each atom.

Lowering the temperature reduces thermal agitation, which normally opposes the alignment of dipoles. However, since all dipoles are already perfectly aligned at saturation, a decrease in temperature cannot increase the alignment any further.

Therefore, the magnetization will remain constant.
